textPlain: "Microsoft shipped a broken “Update and shut down” toggle with Windows 10, and it never acknowledged it until now."I guess they know what's best for the user base, and this was obviously deemed not important.  But boy did they get Copilot integrated in everything post haste.Typical Microsoft hubris.
I can't believe I'm alive to witness this.
Looking at the rest of their recent update history, this likely just broke...Task failed successfully.
That was a bug? I always thought it was because of my dual boot configuration.
Seriously, on a Linux system, I update everything except the kernel without a reboot. Why can’t Windows do this?
Different teams, perhaps. No idea. A monster org like this must be massively disconnected internally, specially for non-critical bugs and such.
Yeah, even with this fixed it's going to be annoying because it first restarts, so you have to stay at the PC to select windows in grub

KDE Neon used to do this. Almost always caused issues after update with stuff crashing due to mismatch of versions talking to each other over D-Bus and such.So they moved to something more like the Windows style, where it downloads, reboots to apply and then reboots again freshly updated.

Windows locks files when they're in use, so that you cannot overwrite them.
Linux doesn't do this.So if you want to overwrite a running service then you can either stop it, update it, and restart it (tricky to manage if it has dependencies, or is necessary for using the PC), or to shut down everything, update the files while the OS isn't (or is barely) running, and then restart the OS.
The file system won’t allow you to overwrite an open file in Windows.
thinking it has something to do with this https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/49306
